data.title: Fact Check: How much money did the University of Chicago actually lose trading cryptocurrency?
data.description:
Author: Darren Terminator

Recently, Jiemian News took the opportunity of the publication of the third edition of Professor Zhao Dingxin's "Lectures on Social and Political Movements" (the second edition of this book is truly excellent) to interview Professor Zhao. In the interview, Professor Zhao stated that the University of Chicago's recent budget cuts were because "it is said that the university seems to have listened to the investment advice of some Nobel laureates, losing more than six billion US dollars in cryptocurrency trading. It can be said that the University of Chicago's reduction in liberal arts programs has nothing to do with Trump's policies."

So, did the University of Chicago really lose more than six billion US dollars in cryptocurrency trading?

Coincidentally, the University of Chicago mentioned cryptocurrency trading in its Q&A updated in December 2025[1]. According to the official website: "Contrary to a certain news report, the University of Chicago has not suffered losses on its cryptocurrency investments. Our investment in cryptocurrencies is relatively small, but it has more than doubled in the past five years. Our investment goal is to provide a stable source of income to support our programs in the long term and secure our future." So, is the University of Chicago's provost necessarily telling the truth? It's hard to say. However, intuitively speaking, the total amount of the University of Chicago's endowment fund in the last five years is about $10 billion (the highest in fiscal year 2021 was about $11.6 billion; and about $10.9 billion in fiscal year 2025 [2]). Unless the University of Chicago is really crazy enough to use at least 60% of its endowment fund to speculate on cryptocurrencies (which obviously violates various regulations), or misappropriates a large amount of operating funds to speculate on cryptocurrencies and loses it all, it should not have lost as much as six billion. So how much did it actually lose? Or did it actually make a fortune as the official Q&A said? The Stanford University newspaper [3], the Financial Times [4] and Investopedia [5] reported on this last year. According to the Stanford C.T. newspaper, their four sources indicated that "the University of Chicago lost tens of millions of dollars around 2021 due to its cryptocurrency investments." So what does the University of Chicago's financial report [6] say? Unfortunately, the financial report does not directly tell us exactly how much money was lost in cryptocurrency trading. However, in its 2022 fiscal year financial report, the University of Chicago disclosed its cryptocurrency investments (fair market value): approximately $64 million as of the end of June 2021, and as of June 2022... At the end of the month, it was approximately $45 million (the difference was approximately $19 million). In subsequent financial reports, perhaps due to excessive profits or losses, the University of Chicago changed its reporting method and stopped disclosing its cryptocurrency investments. However, according to a Q&A in 2025, the University of Chicago remained relatively cautious in its cryptocurrency investments. It's worth noting that the 2022 financial report showed that as of the end of June that year, the University of Chicago's endowment fund had suffered a total investment loss of approximately $1.5 billion. The 2023 financial report showed that the University of Chicago's investments only incurred a small loss. In the following two years, the University of Chicago's investment performance improved. However, we don't know exactly which of these losses and profits originated from cryptocurrency trading. The Stanford University student newspaper offered a less reliable clue: "[The University of Chicago's] target asset allocation data shows that the university's ideal allocation to private debt and 'absolute return' investments (including alternative assets such as cryptocurrencies) has decreased from 25.5% in 2020 to 20% in 2022, suggesting a significant withdrawal (or decline) of high-risk alternative assets." However, the Stanford University student newspaper also offered an interesting observation: "From 2013 to 2023, the University of Chicago's endowment fund had an annualized return of only 7.48%, while the stock market's annualized return was 12.8%, and the Ivy League schools' average was 10.8%. If the University of Chicago had simply followed market performance, its endowment fund would now be $6.45 billion more. And this (dreamlike) sum would be more than enough to pay off all of the school's debts." Of course, universities cannot simply replicate market indices, as they must hedge against economic downturns to maintain financial stability. But even if the University of Chicago were to reach the average level of its Ivy League peers, its endowment would still be $3.69 billion larger. This would be enough to cover the school’s current budget deficit for the next 15 years. However, besides cryptocurrency speculation and investment losses, what other reasons could explain the University of Chicago’s budget cuts? Common explanations, besides Trump being a rogue, often emphasize the University of Chicago’s own strategic mistakes: borrowing heavily, large-scale infrastructure projects, and aggressive expansion. [7][8] As of the end of June 2025, the University of Chicago’s debt was approximately $9.2 billion [9], about 90% of its endowment. Although the financing costs of these debts are relatively low compared to those across the ocean, the University of Chicago still needs to pay more than $200 million in interest this fiscal year. Such a high debt is certainly not out of thin air. Since the beginning of the new century, the University of Chicago has spent a lot of money on new laboratories, libraries, dormitories, and technology in order to enhance its reputation and enrollment, as well as to compete with various established universities. Moreover, these expansions are mostly supported by a large amount of debt. However, the new infrastructure brings continuous operating costs, but the school has not thought about how to support it in the long term. The University of Chicago newspaper[10] quoted Professor Clifford Ando as saying that any parents who want to send their children to the University of Chicago need to think about whether the tuition fees they have worked so hard to pay are paying for their children's education or paying for the school's debt. The crazy expansion and the debt problems that followed are obviously the responsibility of the school's management for being hot-headed and ambitious. Ironically, the president's base salary increased by 285% between 2006 and 2022. Now facing some economic problems, the management is shifting the burden onto students and faculty: even in years of selling assets, laying off staff, and halting enrollment, executive salaries continue to rise. So what should the University of Chicago do next? Besides continuing to cut costs, it must, of course, increase revenue. Clearly, a common tactic used by American universities to increase revenue is to enroll more undergraduates. The University of Chicago will do the same, though its reasons will certainly be presented in a high-sounding manner.

data.title: Behind the silver "short squeeze": Why is shorting it still dangerous?
data.description:
Author: BiyaNews

Recently, the silver market has been as hot as the summer heat. Looking at the SLV (iShares Silver Trust ETF) trend, many investors exclaimed, "This is practically the 'Nvidia' of precious metals!" When an asset rises at such a steep slope, the "fear of heights" and "contrarian thinking" factors in human nature begin to stir. I have friends who are itching to short it, betting on a pullback.

However, historical experience tells me that under the current macroeconomic landscape and microeconomic structure, shorting silver is tantamount to colliding head-on with a high-speed train. You might guess the direction correctly, but you're unlikely to survive until victory. Behind this is far more than just "what goes up must come down"; it's an extreme short squeeze orchestrated by multiple "trump card" factors.

Spot Trading "Flips the Table": When Financial Games Meet Real Demand

Traditionally, the precious metals trading system centered in London and New York has built a vast world of derivatives. In this world, $100 worth of physical silver in a warehouse may correspond to several times or even dozens of times more futures and options contracts. This is essentially a credit-based financial game.

But the rules of the game are being rewritten.

In recent years, physical demanders, represented by major global manufacturing centers, have exhibited a clear attitude: "Forget the abstract, I want physical delivery." When industrial users and investors are no longer satisfied with paper contracts and demand physical delivery, the vulnerability of the entire system is laid bare. According to publicly available market reports and warehousing data, COMEX (New York Mercantile Exchange) silver inventories have shown signs of tightness several times in recent years. Short sellers in the futures market face a dilemma at expiration: either buy silver at a high price for delivery or close their positions. Both actions drive up prices. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle: rising prices → triggering more delivery demand or short covering → further pushing up prices → inventory appears even tighter. This price surge driven by physical delivery pressure is a "black swan" event that is difficult for technical analysis to predict. I recall the short squeeze at GameSpot (GME) in early 2021, triggered by retail investors. Its core logic was similar: a short squeeze. When short positions are too crowded and the available circulating supply (or physical commodity) is insufficient, any buying pressure can trigger a stampede of short covering. While the silver market isn't entirely driven by retail investors, the underlying logic of "spot trading is king" means that any large-scale short selling risks being "physically" disproven. Industrial "Silver Devourers": Demand Restructuring Under AI and the Green Revolution If a financial short squeeze is "adding fuel to the fire," then profound changes in fundamental demand are the "dried tinder" burning ever brighter in the furnace. Silver is not a scarce metal, but its demand structure is undergoing a silent revolution. For a long time, silver has been known for its investment properties and jewelry demand. However, it is easily overlooked that it is also an extremely important industrial metal. Silver possesses the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of all metals, making it irreplaceable in high-end manufacturing. Currently, two major trends are reshaping the industrial demand landscape for silver: Artificial Intelligence and Electrification: The explosive growth of AI data centers, high-performance computing chips, and 5G/6G infrastructure has created massive demand for high-end connectors, switches, and conductors. Every server and every base station is silently consuming silver. Green Energy Revolution: The photovoltaic industry is the absolute growth engine for silver demand. The silver paste on the front of each solar panel is the core conductive material. Despite technological advancements aimed at reducing unit consumption ("finer lines," "less silver"), the exponential growth in global photovoltaic (PV) installations has completely offset these savings. Data from organizations like the International Energy Agency (IEA) continues to revise upwards forecasts for future PV installations, indicating a robust engine for "green demand" for silver. The supply side is equally compelling. More than half of the world's silver is produced as a byproduct of metals such as copper, lead, zinc, and gold. This means that silver supply is not independent and heavily reliant on mining investment and extraction plans for other base metals. When major mining companies slow investment due to costs, environmental policies, or unfavorable expectations for base metal prices, silver supply growth is naturally limited. A more nuanced point is that the world's largest silver producer and a major industrial consumer has shown a stronger strategic control over key mineral resources, including silver, in recent years. While specific policy details require consulting the latest official documents, a major trend is that countries are placing greater emphasis on using resources to safeguard their domestic supply chains rather than simply exporting for foreign exchange. This rise of "resource nationalism," in the long run, may further tighten global silver trade flows and exacerbate regional supply-demand mismatches. The "Hidden" Geopolitical Manifesto: Silver's Return to its Monetary Role? In investment, some of the most powerful drivers are often hidden beneath the headlines. The current complex geopolitical landscape is, in a classical way, reawakening silver's ancient attribute—that of a monetary metal. When traditional international payment systems (such as SWIFT) become unusable due to sanctions, transacting parties must find an alternative medium with high consensus, stable value, and ease of cross-border transfer. Gold is certainly the first choice, but its high unit price is not conducive to small- to medium-sized trade settlements. This is where silver's advantages become apparent. Market analysis points out that in trade between countries restricted by international sanctions and their trading partners, silver is being used as a form of "physical currency" or collateral. For the recipient, silver is both a hard asset with intrinsic value and can be easily integrated into their domestic industrial chain (such as India's huge silver jewelry processing and consumption market) or resold to the global market. This process essentially creates a new "enclave" of physical silver demand within the gaps of international trade, independent of the dollar system. This demand is hidden and difficult to quantify precisely, yet it is real. Like ocean currents beneath the surface, though unseen, it continuously and powerfully influences the direction of the sea. This provides a solid "hidden support" for silver prices, with low correlation to global financial market fluctuations.

Lessons for Investors: Be Afraid of Heights, But Don't Go Against the Trend

Faced with such a complex confluence of positive factors, how should we respond?

First, understand the essence of the market. The current silver market is not simply a speculative bubble, but the result of multiple resonances of financial conditions, industry cycles, and geopolitics. The biggest risk of shorting is that you are fighting against a fundamental situation supported by a real spot shortage, strong industrial demand, and strategic hoarding. This is different from shorting an overvalued tech stock, which may only face the risk of liquidity contraction.

Second, respect the power of trends. My experience is that in the early stages of a trend, you can discuss valuations; but in the mid-to-late stages of a trend's acceleration, especially when accompanied by structural changes (such as the aforementioned spot market run and demand revolution), discussing "where the top is" is often more meaningful than discussing "when the reversal will occur."

Trying to predict how high lava will erupt during a volcanic eruption is dangerous and futile. So, how should ordinary investors participate? For trend traders: Consider ETFs like SLV, but always set strict stop-loss orders. The end of a trend often sees dramatic fluctuations; entry timing and position management are more important than directional judgment. For long-term investors: Consider allocating a small portion of silver as a commodity asset to hedge against currency devaluation and geopolitical risks, in a diversified portfolio, rather than as a tool for short-term speculation. For everyone: If you feel prices are high and you don't know where to start, the best strategy is to wait and see. The market never lacks opportunities, it lacks patience. As the old saying goes: "Missing out" isn't a risk, "making a mistake" is. The story of silver is a microcosm of the clash between the old world's financial order and the new world's industrial demands, and also a vivid lesson in risk education: never short sell simply because prices have "gone too high." The real risk lies in ignoring the underlying forces driving prices up. In the market's frenzy, sometimes the wisest move is neither to join in nor to fight back, but to stand aside and first understand the rhythm of the music. After all, the prerequisite for making money is protecting your principal.

data.title: China Bans More Than A Dozen Western Cybersecurity Companies As It Pushes For Tech Self-Reliance
data.description:
China Moves To Ban Western Cybersecurity Firms Over National Security Concerns

Chinese authorities have instructed domestic companies to stop using cybersecurity software from more than a dozen U.S. and Israeli firms, citing concerns that Western technology could transmit sensitive information abroad.

The move is part of Beijing’s broader drive for technological self-reliance under its “Xinchuang” policy.

China has added domestic AI chips from Huawei and Cambricon to its official government procurement “Xinchuang” list for the first time, steering public sector IT spend toward local GPUs pic.twitter.com/hlaZaBByo8

— The FinanceX (@thefinancexnews) December 10, 2025

Which Companies Are Affected By The Ban

The ban targets major American firms including Broadcom-owned VMware, Palo Alto Networks, Fortinet, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Rapid7, McAfee, Recorded Future, Claroty, and Alphabet-owned Mandiant and Wiz.

Israeli firms on the list include Check Point Software Technologies, Orca Security, Cato Networks, and CyberArk, which was recently acquired by Palo Alto Networks.

Investors in these companies need to pay close attention to the impact of China's ban.

US:Microsoft/Cisco/Palo Alto/Fortinet/CrowdStrike/Broadcom/VMware/Zscaler/Okta
Israel:Check Point/CyberArk/Wiz/Orca Security/Aqua Security/SentinelOne/Cato Networks/Claroty/Cyera/Radware/Snyk https://t.co/6APbckc1Wd

— LimSurb (@limsurb) January 15, 2026

Imperva, now owned by French defence company Thales, is also affected.

Several of the banned firms operate significant local offices.

Fortinet maintains three offices in mainland China and one in Hong Kong, Broadcom has six locations, and Palo Alto Networks operates five offices, including one in Macau.

Other firms, like CrowdStrike and SentinelOne, said they do not sell directly to Chinese clients and would be minimally affected.

CrowdStrike $CRWD and SentinelOne $S : These two are a useful contrast case because Reuters reported both companies said they do not really sell into China, with CrowdStrike saying it does not sell into China and has no offices, hires, or infrastructure there.😅

— VibeCheckInvest (@VibecheckInvest) January 15, 2026

Why China Is Targeting Western Cybersecurity Software

Beijing has expressed growing concern that Western software could be exploited for espionage.

According to sources familiar with the matter said,

“Chinese authorities expressed concern the software could collect and transmit confidential information abroad.”

The move follows repeated allegations by some of the banned companies that Chinese hackers targeted foreign governments and diplomats.

Check Point recently reported a Chinese-linked operation against a European government office, while Palo Alto Networks claimed Chinese hackers targeted diplomats worldwide.

China-linked #InkDragon expands into Europe, building a distributed relay network by weaponizing compromised servers with a custom #ShadowPad IIS listener alongside new TTPs and an evolved FinalDraft.https://t.co/HiVSN2rngc

— Check Point Research (@_CPResearch_) December 16, 2025

China has denied these allegations.

Domestic Alternatives Are Being Prioritised

Local firms are being encouraged to switch to Chinese cybersecurity providers such as 360 Security Technology and Neusoft.

Analysts note that this mirrors actions taken by the U.S., which banned Russian software like Kaspersky Lab from government networks in 2017 and again restricted its products in 2024.

The US is set to join a host of other countries restricting popular antivirus software developer #Kaspersky Lab's software due to concerns about Russian government influence.

Find out more about the ban and how you can better protect your data 👉https://t.co/hnnXeqi4N5pic.twitter.com/MXDsHEHCL7

— Proton (@ProtonPrivacy) August 15, 2024

Impact On Companies And Markets

The announcement affected share prices of some of the blacklisted firms.

Broadcom shares fell more than 5%, Fortinet dropped around 2%, and Palo Alto Networks declined slightly.

Check Point shares rose marginally.

Companies like CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Recorded Future clarified they do business minimally in China, while others maintain extensive operations.

China’s Push For Technology Independence

The ban reflects Beijing’s push to reduce reliance on foreign technology amid growing U.S.-China competition in semiconductors, AI, and cybersecurity.

Chinese state-owned enterprises are also being pressured to limit the use of Western hardware and consulting services.

The timing coincides with preparations for a visit by U.S. President Donald Trump to Beijing in April, highlighting how technological and trade tensions continue to influence China’s domestic policies.

NEW: President Trump says he’ll be visiting China in April — and announces that President Xi will come to the U.S. soon after. pic.twitter.com/CFVFd8DbWu

— Fox News (@FoxNews) October 30, 2025

China’s strategy demonstrates a clear focus on national security and technological sovereignty, steering domestic companies away from foreign software and encouraging investment in local alternatives.

data.title: How to value a public blockchain
data.description:
Author: Donovan Choy Source: onchaintimes Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Introduction

The crypto industry is keen to debate the valuation logic of public blockchains, and there are good reasons behind this. Firstly, in the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies of $3 trillion, public blockchain tokens still account for approximately 84%, making their valuation importance self-evident.

Secondly, although the industry claims to have entered the "mainstream" stage, even the top investors in the industry have consistently failed to reach a consensus on the valuation of public blockchains. One group of investors adheres to the fundamental investment philosophy, emphasizing the importance of tangible cash flow indicators and price-to-earnings ratios (P/E). For example, Jon Charbonneau of DBA suggests applying the commonly used P/E ratio in the technology industry to the "Real Economic Value (REV)" indicator to value various public blockchains. Real economic value is an indicator similar to Gross Domestic Product (GDP), used to measure the total amount of transaction fees and tips generated by all transactions on a public blockchain. Another group consists of staunch supporters of Bitcoin and Ethereum, who believe in the "currency premium" theory. These investors argue that cash flow metrics severely underestimate the value of public blockchain networks and oppose simply equating public blockchain tokens with equity instruments. In their view, the value of public blockchain tokens lies in many intangible elements that are difficult to quantify, such as the token's "monetary attributes," network effects, degree of decentralization, and value storage function. A recent debate between Santiago of Inversion and Haseeb of Dragonfly is a classic microcosm of this public blockchain valuation debate. The core arguments are roughly as follows: Santiago: The current price-to-earnings ratios of public blockchain tokens are ridiculously high and should have fallen significantly long ago. Haseeb: But talking about P/E ratios and cash flow now is completely unrealistic – the wave of exponential growth is just around the corner! (It needs to be clarified that Haseeb is not a pure “absolute supporter of currency premiums,” but he clearly agrees with the view that “it is too early to judge public chains using traditional indicators.”) Reconciling the Valuation Methods of the Two Factions: Is there a valuation model that satisfies both sides? Can we introduce rational economic analysis while avoiding getting bogged down in vague conceptual debates like “currency premiums”? Recently, at Breakpoint, a valuation model proposed by Jon Ma, CEO of Artemis, seems to have achieved this goal. This model is essentially a fusion of two classic valuation methods: A hybrid valuation framework used by venture capital firms to evaluate early-stage tech network platforms (such as Uber and Amazon) The quantity theory of money (MV = PQ) SOL Valuation Case Study Jon Ma applied this model to the valuation of SOL, yielding astonishing results. I made slight adjustments to his model, and the final calculated price range for the SOL token is shown in the chart below. After substituting SOL's data into the model, it was calculated that its token could be valued at $1,000 in 2030, but this figure is not the most surprising part. What truly caught my attention was that the model calculated the fair value of the SOL token at approximately $83, while its current market price is $135, a remarkably close difference. Here is the core logic behind the model's projection that the SOL token will reach $1,000 by 2030: 1. According to a report by the Boston Consulting Group (BCG), the total potential market size (TAM) of tokenized assets worldwide is projected to reach $9 trillion by 2030. 2. Using stablecoin market capitalization as a benchmark, Solana currently accounts for 2.72% of this market size (corresponding to a market capitalization of $16 billion). Assuming Solana can reliably capture 5% of the total potential market size by 2030. 3. Based on this ratio, by 2030, the market capitalization/assets under management (AUM) of stablecoins within the Solana ecosystem will reach $450 billion (Note: This refers to assets under management, not total value locked (TVL)). 4. Currently, the velocity of money on Solana is approximately 180. It is reasonable to infer that as more RWAs are tokenized on-chain, these assets will have lower liquidity, slower turnover, and generate significantly less trading volume than assets like memes. Therefore, the model assumes that by 2030, Solana's velocity of money will drop to approximately 60 (the current velocity of money on Tron is in the 80-90 range). 5. If the velocity of circulation drops to 60, the corresponding on-chain transaction volume will reach $27 trillion. Based on a 0.1% transaction fee, Solana will generate $27 billion in revenue. (For reference, Solana's annualized real economic value in 2025—a metric criticized by Ethereum supporters—is approximately $1.4 billion, only about half of the model's projected $2.9 billion). 6. Assuming a 15% profit margin in the Software as a Service (SaaS) industry, and deducting Solana's annual basic operating costs of $900 million, the net profit would be approximately $22.1 billion. 7. Applying a price-to-earnings ratio of 30, the estimated value of the SOL token in 2030 would be approximately $1,000. Okay, so another public blockchain valuation model tells you that the public blockchain tokens you favor will skyrocket within a decade. But what's the point? The real highlight of this model isn't the valuation figures it calculates, but the logical process behind them. It may be the first valuation model to find a balance between "fundamental" and "currency premium" investors. Fundamental investors accept this model because it uses a price-to-earnings ratio framework, analyzing public blockchains as Software as a Service (SaaS) companies in the Web2 era, without forcing them to accept concepts like "Ethereum/Solana is currency." For currency premium investors, this model calculates revenue using the indicator "total transaction volume (assets under management × velocity of circulation)," explicitly incorporating the elusive currency premium into the pricing system. This is tantamount to acknowledging that even idle funds sitting on the blockchain (such as stablecoins and tokenized real-world assets), even without directly generating transaction fees or MEV, should have a place in a reasonable valuation model. The next question is this multi-billion dollar issue: what would happen if this model were applied to Ethereum? Frankly, Ethereum's short-term valuation prospects are relatively bleak. The model calculates that the current fair value of Ethereum tokens is approximately $948 (don't be surprised yet), but starting in 2029, its valuation will enter a rapid upward trend, potentially reaching $7,200, and even challenging the legendary $10,000 mark in 2030. Based on Ethereum's current price of approximately $3,000, this translates to a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 36.06% over the next five years—a truly impressive achievement! More importantly, this valuation result coincides with a growing view that cryptocurrency valuations may mirror the dot-com bubble: a prolonged period of stagnation followed by a sustained upward trend. To adapt Jon Ma's model to Ethereum's valuation, I made the following adjustments: Lower velocity of circulation: I set Ethereum's velocity of circulation to be reduced by 50% significantly more than Solana's (10%). The logic behind this is simple: Ethereum is a more mature public chain, with most funds on the chain being "dormant" collateral, resulting in a lower turnover rate. Ethereum has long since moved beyond the "meme casino" stage and is steadily entering the "digital bond" development phase. Lower transaction fee rate: Set at 0.05%, lower than Solana's 0.1%. This reflects Ethereum's "landlord" role – focusing on handling high-value settlement business for institutions, obtaining stable but meager "rent" by setting a floor price for block space fees (Blob Fee), while shifting a large number of ordinary transactions to Layer 2 (L2) networks for processing. Lower operating costs: According to the latest disclosed data, the Ethereum Foundation's annual operating costs are only $150 million, far lower than Solana's $900 million operating costs.

  • Mild Deflationary Effect: Considering Ethereum's recent "Fusaka" hard fork—which fixed the previous inflationary loophole by linking the floor price of block space fees to Ethereum's underlying transaction fees—the model assumes that Ethereum's circulating supply will exhibit a mild deflationary trend.

    • Finally, I'd like to add a point: I considered including WBTC and WETH in the "Amount Under Management" statistics, but ultimately abandoned the idea. While they do technically play the role of "currency" in protocols like Maker Vault, including them in assets under management would create a problem—a circular reasoning paradox. Bitcoin price fluctuations directly lead to fluctuations in assets under management, thus making the valuation of public chain tokens entirely dependent on market sentiment fluctuations.

    Conclusion

    Jon Ma's model seems to have found a reasonable balance between the two major schools of thought among investors, although I believe they can still find many points of disagreement. If you are a value investor, this model provides the dual assurance of a price-to-earnings ratio framework and balance sheet discipline, giving you more confidence when investing.

    If you do not accept real economic value indicators, believe in the theory of currency premiums, or believe that BlackRock's BUIDL tokenized fund will greatly benefit your crypto assets, then this model also fully respects the structural value of tokenization and network effects.

    If you are a long-term cryptocurrency investor and agree with Haseeb's belief in "power-law growth," this model also leaves ample room for exponential growth. But most importantly, this is a valuation model that can be understood and accepted by Wall Street.

data.title: The battle over stablecoin yields intensifies, with CLARITY becoming the focal point of the crypto-bank rivalry.
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

The Senate Agriculture Committee plans to release its cryptocurrency market structure bill (the CLARITY Act) on January 21 and hold a hearing on its review on January 27. The cryptocurrency industry is paying close attention to this bill because it will define how the country's market regulators—the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission—will regulate the cryptocurrency market.

On January 15, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong stated that after reviewing the Senate Banking Committee's draft cryptocurrency bill, Coinbase could not support the version of legislation. Key issues include: a de facto ban on tokenized stocks; restrictions on DeFi, granting the government unlimited access to personal financial records and weakening privacy rights; weakening the CFTC's authority, stifling innovation, and subordinating it to the SEC; and related amendments that could stifle stablecoin reward mechanisms and allow banks to block competitors. Armstrong pointed out that the draft is "worse than maintaining the status quo," preferring no bill to a bad one, but he remains optimistic about reaching a more reasonable version through continued collaboration and emphasized that the crypto industry should receive the same regulatory treatment in the United States as traditional finance.

What controversies currently exist surrounding this bill? Who supports it, and who opposes it? When will the bill be implemented? What are its specific contents?

I. Controversies surrounding the CLARITY Act

The content regarding stablecoin yields in the CLARITY Act has become a focal point of debate between cryptocurrency companies and banking groups.

Banking groups argue that stablecoin products offering yields are similar to deposit-taking or unregulated investment vehicles. Last week, a group of American community bankers urged Congress to amend the GENIUS Act, arguing that stablecoin issuers are exploiting a loophole that allows yields to be indirectly passed to token holders through exchanges and other partners.

Bankers warn that reward programs offered by cryptocurrency exchanges could siphon billions of dollars from community banks, eroding their ability to lend to small businesses, farmers, students, and homebuyers. Banking advocates say allowing stablecoins as rewards could hit them even harder; the Treasury Department estimated last April that widespread adoption of stablecoins could drain $6.6 trillion from the traditional banking system. Cryptocurrency companies, however, argue that such programs are more akin to points rewards or payment incentives common in the fintech space. The Cryptocurrency Innovation Council and the Blockchain Association rejected the banks' proposals in a letter to the Senate Banking Committee, arguing that “paying stablecoins is not intended to fund loans” and that these revisions would stifle innovation and consumer choice. If the bill passes without favorable provisions, it could have a significant impact on Coinbase's balance sheet, as the exchange earned $247 million from stablecoin revenue and an additional $154.8 million from blockchain rewards in the fourth quarter. Columbia Business School adjunct professor Malecan believes that the US banking industry, in order to protect its own interests, has been spreading a "myth" about stablecoin yields. Congress should prioritize consumers, not profitable banks. The legislation now seems to depend in part on whether stablecoin issuers should be able to share their economic data with third parties. The main conflict lies in the "yield bottleneck," i.e., who can profit from the interest on stablecoin reserves. First, the view that the growth of stablecoins will only lead to a decrease in bank deposits is wrong: stablecoins may actually increase bank deposits because most of the demand for stablecoins comes from overseas. Since issuers must hold Treasury bills and bank deposits as reserves, this will promote the growth of overall banking activity. Second, stablecoin competition will not harm lending business, only bank profits. Banks can compete by paying depositors higher interest rates. II. Who Opposes, Who Supports? 1. Opposition Voices Coinbase is the biggest opponent of the bill. If the bill restricts stablecoin issuers from offering rewards on cryptocurrency exchanges and other platforms, Coinbase "may reconsider its support for the bill." Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said Wednesday that he had reviewed the draft text of the Senate banking bill but "unfortunately, he cannot support the current version of the bill." "There are too many problems, including effectively banning tokenized stocks, banning decentralized finance (DeFi), giving the government unlimited access to your financial records, stripping you of your privacy, weakening the power of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), stifling innovation and subordinating it to the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), and the draft amendments would kill stablecoin rewards and allow banks to ban their competitors." Armstrong thanked Senate members for their hard work in reaching a bipartisan consensus, "but this version would be substantially worse than the current situation. We would rather have no bill than receive a bad one." Ryan Rasmussen, head of research at Bitwise Invest, expressed a similar view, stating that the current CLARITY bill draft is detrimental to tokenization, stablecoins, DeFi, privacy, developers, users, investors, and innovation. Venture capitalist Tim Draper also voiced his support for Coinbase CEO Armstrong, stating, "Armstrong makes a good point. The current Senate compromise is worse than no bill at all. It sounds like banks are interfering." The cryptocurrency community is also fighting back, with the "Stand with Crypto" organization claiming its supporters have sent over 135,000 emails to senators to protect stablecoin rewards. 2. Voices of Support Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Paul Atkins: “This bill aligns with the president’s strategic focus on making the U.S. the world’s cryptocurrency capital. Therefore, with clear legislation and rules, the market gains certainty. We fully support it and are very optimistic that this bill will be signed into law by the president this year. I believe this will be a huge help to the cryptocurrency market.” Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott: “This bill provides ordinary Americans with the protection and certainty they deserve. Investors and innovators cannot continue to wait while Washington does nothing and criminals exploit loopholes. This bill puts the interests of ordinary people first, cracks down on criminals and foreign adversaries, and keeps the future of finance in the United States.” Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse stated that he remains “optimistic that these issues can be resolved through the markup process. This is a significant step towards providing a viable framework for cryptocurrencies while continuing to protect consumer rights; the success of this bill is the success of cryptocurrencies.” III. When Will It Be Implemented? Justin Slaughter, Vice President of Regulatory Affairs at Paradigm, stated that the implementation of the Cryptocurrency Market Structure Act could take many years due to the lengthy rule-making process. Even if the House and Senate pass the bill and President Donald Trump signs it into law, it could still take nearly two presidential terms for all the rules to take effect. Because “this bill alone requires the drafting of 45 rules,” “the implementation process is likely to extend beyond this presidential term and potentially into the next.” Investment bank TD Cowen stated that the midterm elections could weaken the support needed to pass the bill, making it more likely to pass in 2027 and be finally implemented in 2029.

Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Paul Atkins stated that he is confident the Cryptocurrency Market Structure Act will be submitted to President Donald Trump for signature this year. The most important thing the government can do for investors right now is "to bring the crypto asset market out of the regulatory gray area. Bipartisan market structure legislation will help us guard against unscrupulous regulators and ensure we achieve President Trump's goal of making the U.S. the world's cryptocurrency capital."

Appendix: Key Points of the CLARITY Act

  • Clearly Distinguishing Between Securities and Commodities: The Act defines "auxiliary assets" (tokens) and, based on the principles of decentralization and control, specifies when these assets should be considered digital commodities rather than securities.

    • Auxiliary assets are network tokens whose value depends on the entrepreneurial or managerial efforts of the auxiliary asset originator (original issuer or creator) or related personnel.

      • Auxiliary assets are network tokens whose value depends on the entrepreneurial or managerial efforts of the auxiliary asset originator (original issuer or creator) or related personnel.

        • Auxiliary assets are network tokens whose value depends on the entrepreneurial or managerial efforts of the auxiliary asset originator (original issuer or creator) or related personnel.

          • The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is responsible for clearly defining the specific composition of ancillary assets. Disclosure Requirements for Crypto Projects: Crypto asset issuers must disclose comprehensive information to the SEC regarding their projects, financial resources, and development until the network is certified as sufficiently decentralized. Strict Combating Illicit Fund Flows: The Bank Secrecy Act explicitly extends to cryptocurrency service providers (brokers, dealers, exchanges) to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, including stricter regulations for cryptocurrency ATMs. Protection for Pure Software Developers: The draft explicitly states that individuals who merely develop code, release code, or operate verification services (without controlling assets) should not be regulated as financial intermediaries. Integration into the Banking System: Banks and credit unions can provide cryptocurrency custody services, issue stablecoins, and use distributed ledger technology in their operations, provided it is done in a manner that complies with security regulations. Stablecoin Regulation: Rules have been established for "payment stablecoins," including prohibiting service providers from paying interest solely for holding stablecoins, in order to maintain their nature as a means of payment (not an investment). Regulatory Sandbox Establishment: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission will jointly create a "mini innovation sandbox" that allows companies to test innovative blockchain products under supervision and enjoy limited regulatory exemptions. Legal Certainty for NFTs: A "safe harbor" has been created for non-fungible tokens (NFTs), explicitly stating that NFTs are generally not securities when they constitute artwork, collectibles, or access rights.

        • Customer Protection in Bankruptcy: This draft amendment revises bankruptcy law to ensure that customers' crypto assets are protected as customer property and do not fall into the bankruptcy estate in the event of the collapse of intermediaries (such as exchanges).

        • Regulation of DeFi Protocols: This draft attempts to regulate decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols by distinguishing between truly decentralized systems and "shared control" systems, and by introducing voluntary cybersecurity standards.

data.title: The core principles of cryptocurrency value: A review of leading players in major profit-generating sectors
data.description:
Author: Stacy Muur Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The initial "fat protocol" theory held that value in the cryptocurrency space would tilt towards the underlying public chains, rather than various applications. However, this framework no longer holds true.

In 2025, I observed that value was flowing towards various "value control points": user interfaces that control user needs, trading platforms that aggregate liquidity, issuers that hold balance sheets, and network layers that profit from market inefficiencies. Regardless of which public chain ultimately prevails, which application becomes popular, or which narrative dominates, these entities can continuously earn transaction fee revenue.

This ranking combines multiple indicators such as revenue, user scale, average revenue per user (ARPU), market share, and capital efficiency to analyze the actual value flow in the current market, clarifying which ecosystem layers possess "value depth," the underlying reasons, and the next direction of marginal value flow.

I. Leading Wallets Leader: Phantom Annualized Revenue: Approximately $105 million (Q3 2025: Quarterly revenue approximately $35 million, annualized) Monthly Active Users: Approximately 15 million Average Annual Revenue Per User: Approximately $7.00 Market Position: Holds approximately 39% of the Solana wallet market share Performance and Theoretical Alignment: Phantom, by controlling the entry point of user needs, has become... The leading user-facing wallet in the Solana ecosystem. This wallet covers diverse scenarios including token exchange, NFT trading, perpetual contracts, and payments, enabling the monetization of user behavior before its value is transferred to decentralized exchanges or other protocols. Within weeks of its launch, Phantom's perpetual contracts achieved a trading volume exceeding $1 billion, demonstrating the wallet's transformation from a passive interactive interface to a proactive financial services platform. In January 2025, Phantom completed a $150 million Series C funding round, reaching a valuation of $3 billion, reflecting market recognition of its transformation strategy. Following closely behind are the following competitors: MetaMask: Expanding monetization channels by integrating perpetual contracts and token exchange functionality, and launching a $30 million LINEA ecosystem incentive program to further deepen user ecosystem stickiness. Trust Wallet: With over 200 million total downloads and having blocked $162 million in fraud attacks, demonstrating strong user reach, but its average revenue per user is relatively weak.

II. Leading Public Chains

Leader: Ethereum

  • Protocol Annualized Revenue: Approximately $300 million

  • Monthly Active Users: Approximately 8.6 million

  • Average Annual Revenue per User: Approximately $30-35

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:
Ethereum remains the core settlement layer in the cryptocurrency field. Its value does not stem from its high-throughput client-side transaction processing capabilities, but rather from its role as the final arbiter for high-value transactions, MEV withdrawals, stablecoin circulation, and various rolling network and institutional financial settlements. Ethereum's fee revenue comes from MEV (Mean Exchange Value), block space fees, and settlement requirements, rather than simply the number of transactions. This characteristic structurally slows its growth compared to public chains focused on transaction execution, but with continued capital inflows, its competitive advantage is becoming increasingly robust. Following closely behind are: Solana: A leading transaction execution public chain, with peak monthly revenue reaching $240 million. Its growth is driven by meme trading, perpetual contracts, and various user-facing applications. The implementation of performance upgrades such as Firedancer and Alpenglow further solidifies its momentum. Base: The fastest-growing Ethereum Layer 2 network by activity level, with transaction volume achieving triple-digit growth month-over-month. Uniswap's cumulative transaction volume has surpassed $200 billion, making it the core transaction execution platform for Ethereum users.

III. Leading Decentralized Exchanges for Perpetual Contracts

Leader: Hyperliquid

  • Annualized Revenue: Approximately $950-1 billion

  • Open Interest: Approximately $6.5 billion

  • 30-Day Perpetual Contract Trading Volume: Approximately $225 Billion

Performance and Theoretical Fit: Perpetual contracts are the most profitable basic product in the cryptocurrency field, and Hyperliquid firmly occupies a dominant position in this sector.

Performance and Theoretical Fit: Perpetual contracts are the most profitable basic product in the cryptocurrency field, and Hyperliquid firmly occupies a dominant position in this field ... This platform utilizes a dedicated public blockchain to achieve integrated aggregation of liquidity, transaction execution, and order flow. It eliminates MEV loss and order routing fragmentation issues during transactions, thus achieving efficient fee capture. In July 2025 alone, Hyperliquid's fee revenue accounted for approximately 35% of the total revenue of all blockchain protocols, and its token buyback scale ranked first among all crypto projects, making it a best practice example of the "thick trading platform" theory. Challenger: Lighter: Rapidly accumulated users from its initial launch, with cumulative trading volume exceeding $1 trillion and monthly trading volume reaching $300 billion, but its profit margin is relatively low. Drift: Cumulative trading volume has exceeded $2 trillion, total value locked (TVL) is approximately $3.2 billion, and revenue is approximately $49 million. It has strong growth momentum, but its market dominance is slightly weaker.

IV. Leading Lending Protocols

Leader: Aave

  • Annualized Revenue: Approximately $115 million

  • Monthly Active Users: Approximately 120,000

  • Total Value Locked: Approximately $32-35 billion

  • Capital Utilization: Approximately 40%

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:

Aave is a leading institutional-grade balance sheet lending platform in DeFi. ... Although the profit margin of lending is structurally lower than that of trading platforms, Aave has formed a unique competitive advantage due to its scale, risk resistance, and high stickiness of institutional funds. In 2025, the protocol's cumulative deposits exceeded $3 trillion, and active lending reached $29 billion. While the accumulation of "value depth" in the lending sector is slow, it possesses extremely strong defensive capabilities. Challenger: Fluid: A leading liquidity protocol with a total cross-chain locked value of $5-6 billion, ranking third in the industry in lending and second in monthly active users. It also provides efficient trading support for decentralized exchanges, with related trading volume reaching $150 billion and generating over $23 million in fees. Morpho: With deposits exceeding $10 billion, it is the largest deposit protocol in the Base ecosystem, indicating that the lending business is transforming towards modularization and marketization. V. Leading Real-World Asset Tokenization Protocols Leader: BlackRock BUIDL Assets Under Management: Approximately $2.3 billion Yield: Approximately 4% (Tokenized US Treasury Products) Number of Holders: Less than 100 (All Institutional Investors) Performance and Theoretical Alignment: The "value depth" of the RWA tokenization track stems from scale and trust, not the number of users. BUIDL has completed deployments across seven public chains, and its related products have been included in the collateral scope of centralized platforms, becoming a structural bridge connecting traditional finance and on-chain finance. This refers to the "value depth" of the balance sheet, not the value accumulation for the user end. Following closely behind are: Ondo Finance: Total value locked has exceeded $1 billion, and it has received approval from the EU's Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA), becoming a leading crypto-native real-world asset tokenization platform.

VI. Leading Liquidity Staking Derivatives / Restaking Protocol Layers

Leader: EigenLayer

  • Restaking Asset Size: Approximately $12.4 billion

  • Annualized Fee Revenue: Approximately $70 million

  • User Base: Approximately 300,000-400,000

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:
EigenLayer is an infrastructure-level protocol in the restaking field, commercializing it by renting out Ethereum's security mechanisms to various Active Validation Services (AVS). The launch of EigenCloud (including EigenAI and EigenCompute) expands its business scope into the field of verifiable off-chain computing. The "value depth" of EigenLayer stems from its strong ecosystem appeal as a protocol. Challenger: Ether.fi: Annualized revenue of approximately $100 million, actively conducting ETHFI token buybacks, and achieving efficient commercial monetization for users through its Cash product.

VII. Leading Aggregators / Routing Protocol Layer

Leader: Jupiter

  • Protocol Annualized Revenue: Approximately $12 million

  • Decentralized Exchange Aggregator 30-Day Trading Volume: Approximately $46 billion

  • Market Share: Accounts for approximately 90% of the traffic of aggregators in the Solana ecosystem

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:
The core of aggregator profitability lies in the trading decision-making process. Jupiter captures value by controlling trading routing, pricing, and execution quality, intercepting profits before liquidity providers obtain the spread. Challenger: COWSwap: With a cumulative trading volume exceeding $1.1 trillion, it possesses strong MEV protection capabilities and is particularly favored by institutional traders.

VIII. Leading Stablecoin Issuers

Leader: Tether (USDT)

  • Circulating Supply: Approximately $185 billion

  • Annualized Revenue: Over $10 billion

  • Treasury Holdings: Approximately $135 billion

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:
Tether is the most profitable entity in the cryptocurrency field. Stablecoin issuers earn interest income by holding US Treasury bonds, realizing the commercialization of floating funds. Their business model is structurally superior to most crypto protocols. This is the purest practical example of the "thick balance sheet" theory. Challengers: USDC (issued by Circle): Circulating supply of approximately $78 billion, rapid growth, but relatively low profit margins. Ethena USDe: Circulating supply of approximately $12 billion, representing a new competitive model for stablecoins based on synthetic yields.

IX. Leading Prediction Markets

Leader: Polymarket

  • Annualized Revenue: Not Disclosed

  • Monthly Trading Volume: Approximately $1.5-2 billion (peaks during major events)

  • Monthly Active Traders: Approximately 200,000-300,000

Performance and Theoretical Alignment:
The core profitability of prediction markets lies in user attention and market uncertainty. Their core structural advantage lies in information attraction—liquidity converges on platforms perceived as having the most accurate pricing. Once a positive cycle of "accurate pricing - attracting liquidity" is formed, competitors will find it difficult to accumulate sufficient market depth. Polymarket's "value depth" doesn't stem from continued user engagement, but rather from its status as a globally influential "consensus bellwether," a level of attention with immense commercial value. Prediction markets represent a new "value depth" in the crypto space, characterized by: not relying on total value locked; not bearing directional market risk; high fee elasticity during major events; and strong narrative dissemination capabilities (market probability data itself can become news hotspots). This characteristic makes it one of the few crypto applications that can benefit from macroeconomic and political fluctuations.

Strong Challenger:

  • Kalshi: Regulated by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), it supports dollar-denominated event trading (such as sporting events and political elections), and its trading volume exceeds that of Polymarket at certain times, attracting the attention of traditional financial institutions. However, it is currently at a disadvantage in terms of crypto-native liquidity.

    Strong Challenger:

    • Kalshi: Regulated by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), it supports dollar-denominated event trading (such as sporting events and political elections). Its ...

    10. Leading MEV Protocol Layers

    Leader: Flashbots

    • Annualized MEV Extraction: Approximately $230 million

    • Cumulative MEV Managed: Over $1.5 billion

    Performance and Theoretical Alignment MEV is the implicit cost of blockchain space. Flashbots institutionalizes the MEV extraction and allocation process, becoming an indispensable infrastructure for Ethereum and various rolling networks.

Performance and Theoretical Alignment MEV is the implicit cost of blockchain space. Flashbots institutionalizes the MEV extraction and allocation process, becoming an indispensable infrastructure for Ethereum and various rolling networks.

Emerging Competitors: Jito: In Q1 2025, it captured approximately 66% of the Solana ecosystem's transaction fee revenue through MEV tips and the Block Space Auction (BAM) mechanism. Arbitrum: Since its launch, it has generated approximately $10 million in transaction fees, marking the expansion of MEV commercialization into higher layers of the ecosystem.

data.title: Polygon Shifts from Layer-2 to Regulated Payment Platform In The United States After Acquiring Coinme And Sequence
data.description:
Polygon Jumps From Layer-2 To Regulated Payments

Polygon, long known for powering Ethereum’s scalability and supporting DeFi and NFT ecosystems, is pivoting from being purely a blockchain infrastructure provider to a fully regulated crypto payments platform in the United States. The platform now aims to make everyday crypto payments and stablecoin transfers fully compliant with U.S. financial rules, signalling a major step toward mainstream adoption.

BREAKING: Polygon to become U.S. regulated payments platform

We’re acquiring Coinme and Sequence to move all money onchain.
→ Regulated money movement in 48 states
→ Fiat on/off ramps
→ 50,000 fiat-to-crypto locations in the U.S.
→ Easy onboarding with wallet infra
→… pic.twitter.com/lwvLheEc3P

— Polygon | POL (@0xPolygon) January 13, 2026

Polygon’s pivot relies on two strategic acquisitions. The purchase of Coinme, a U.S.-based regulated crypto payments firm which gives Polygon access to existing money transmission licenses and the acquisition of Sequence, a blockchain payment infrastructure specialist, strengthens its technical stack for operating regulated payment flows.

Bread talks about Polygon acquiring Coinme and Sequence

"It is the first time a chain co. has bought a regulated entity to his knowledge"

"It was actually kind of groundbreaking" pic.twitter.com/aMXBUFsqim

— Arjun (@clipsofcrypto) January 14, 2026

According to Polygon Labs’ CEO, the acquisition of these two companies provide the “missing pieces” necessary for it to deliver complete, end-to-end regulated crypto payments.

Through these moves, Polygon now holds money transmitter licenses in 48 U.S. states, enabling it to offer large-scale fiat-to-crypto conversion and services compliant with KYC and AML standards. These licenses are crucial because they legally allow Polygon to operate as a payments platform, ensuring customer verification and anti-fraud safeguards are enforced across its transactions.

Stablecoins at the heart of the strategy

At the core of Polygon’s payments push are stablecoins, which provide a predictable and low-volatility medium of exchange for both consumer and business transactions. The platform aims to harness the rapid growth of stablecoin adoption, especially for cross-border payments and B2B settlements, where volatility from assets like Bitcoin or Ether would be a barrier.

🔥 HUGE: Stablecoin market cap hits a new record high of $310 billion, marking a 70% surge in just one year, signaling a fundamental shift in crypto adoption.

What's driving this explosive growth? pic.twitter.com/0qHAaVzIBi

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) December 26, 2025

By embedding stablecoin rails within a regulated framework, Polygon positions itself to serve not only crypto-native users but also traditional financial institutions and fintech companies seeking compliant on-chain infrastructure. This approach aligns with Polygon’s long-term vision of an “Open Money Stack”, enabling value to flow as seamlessly as data across applications and networks.

Polygon’s U.S. focus is also strategic. As the world’s largest financial market with stringent regulatory standards, establishing a compliant footprint in the U.S. provides credibility and opens access to a broad market of institutional and retail participants.

stablecoins now account for 1.35% of the total U.S. dollar supply pic.twitter.com/PVSiMvvdCC

— haonan (@haonan) December 30, 2025

Furthermore, recent regulatory clarity for stablecoins under the GENIUS Act creates a framework for “payment stablecoins,” specifying authorized issuers and operational safeguards. By building in the U.S., Polygon positions itself as a front-runner in regulated crypto payments, ready to capitalize on a booming market at the intersection of blockchain and traditional finance.

data.title: Banks Is Taking Over "Crypto Exchanges" In Europe. How Does This Work?
data.description:
Banks, not exchanges, are rolling out crypto in Europe

DZ Bank, Germany’s second-largest banking group by assets, has secured regulatory approval under the European Union’s Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) — a move that underscores a broader shift underway in Europe’s crypto market: traditional banks are emerging as the main architects of regulated crypto access, rather than crypto-native exchanges.

JUST IN

🇩🇪 Germany’s second largest bank, DZ Bank, gets approval to offer Bitcoin and crypto trading.

TradFi keeps stepping in 👀 pic.twitter.com/edUg5Yp8vl

— Crypto Verse (@CryptoVerse_Co) January 14, 2026

The license, granted by Germany’s financial regulator BaFin, authorizes DZ Bank to operate its “meinKrypto” platform — not as a consumer-facing exchange, but as centralized crypto infrastructure for Germany’s nationwide cooperative banking network.

Rather than competing directly with crypto exchanges, DZ Bank is rolling out crypto services to hundreds of local banks, so they too can provide it to their customers within a fully regulated banking environment. The approach reflects how MiCA is reshaping crypto adoption across Europe: crypto is being absorbed into existing banking rails, not built outside of them.

A hub-and-spoke model for bank-led crypto access

Under the operating structure, DZ Bank acts as the central platform operator, providing the trading, custody, and compliance infrastructure, while individual cooperative banks decide independently whether to offer crypto services to their retail clients.

Each participating bank must still submit a separate MiCA notification to BaFin before activating crypto trading — a safeguard that preserves local control while maintaining centralized regulatory standards. This hub-and-spoke model allows crypto to scale through the banking system without forcing uniform adoption across all institutions.

At launch, meinKrypto will support Bitcoin, Ether, Litecoin, and Cardano, accessible directly through the VR Banking App as a self-directed investment option.

Germany’s 2nd-largest bank just went full crypto. 🇩🇪 DZ Bank launches meinKrypto under MiCAR approval — offering BTC, ETH, ADA & LTC directly to retail customers.#Germany#Bitcoin#Ethereum#Cardano#Litecoinhttps://t.co/uhqX9c6YBz

— TheUnhashed (@theunhashed) January 15, 2026

The platform was a project jointly developed with Atruvia, the IT provider for Germany’s cooperative banking sector, ensuring scalability and regulatory alignment across hundreds of institutions.

Why this matters for Europe’s crypto landscape

DZ Bank’s approval highlights a defining feature of Europe’s crypto trajectory under MiCA: regulated banks — not exchanges — are becoming the primary distribution channel for digital assets.

Unlike crypto-native platforms, banks already possess customer relationships, compliance infrastructure, and balance-sheet capacity. MiCA gives them the legal certainty to integrate crypto gradually, starting with spot trading and custody before expanding into more complex use cases.

While meinKrypto does not currently offer lending, industry observers note that regulated trading and custody are prerequisites for bank-led crypto lending, particularly where assets may later be used as collateral or held on balance sheets. MiCA establishes the guardrails needed for such products to emerge within traditional finance.

From pilots to execution under MiCA

The license builds on DZ Bank’s September 2024 partnership with Boerse Stuttgart Digital, which aimed to roll out crypto trading and custody services across roughly 700 cooperative banks, pending regulatory clearance.

JUST IN: 🇩🇪 Germany’s €370 BILLION DekaBank is launching Bitcoin and crypto trading + custody for institutions.

Their government sells BTC. Their citizens stack it. pic.twitter.com/ogRM96nH2Y

— Dear Bitcoiner ⚡️ (@DearBitcoiner) February 25, 2025

With MiCA authorization now in place, those plans can move from experimentation to execution — positioning DZ Bank among the first major European institutions to operationalize the regulation at scale.

As more EU banks follow suit, MiCA is steadily transforming crypto from a parallel financial system into an embedded feature of Europe’s regulated banking ecosystem, with banks — not exchanges — increasingly setting the terms of adoption.

data.title: Bitpanda Drops London Over Low Liquidity and Opts for Frankfurt IPO in 2026
data.description:
Bitpanda Targets Frankfurt For A High Stakes Public Listing

Bitpanda started in a Vienna office in 2014 and is now ready to face the intense scrutiny of the Frankfurt Stock Exchange.

The company is finalising plans to go public in the first half of 2026, seeking a valuation between 4 billion euros and 5 billion euros.

📣 NEW: Peter Thiel-backed #Bitpanda GmbH is preparing for an IPO on the Frankfurt stock exchange, potentially as early as the first half of 2026. pic.twitter.com/3ufCGUWuvX

— Coinpaper (@coinpapercom) January 14, 2026

This transition from a private fintech unicorn to a listed entity marks a new era for the European crypto market.

While the listing could happen as early as the first quarter, the company is currently working with major banks including Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, and Deutsche Bank AG to manage the process.

Although final decisions on the exact timing are still being discussed, the internal push for a listing is moving quickly as the platform looks to solidify its status as a top player in the region.

Why Bitpanda Chose Frankfurt Over London And New York

Choosing the right venue for an IPO is a strategic gamble, and for Bitpanda, London was simply not an attractive enough option.

Co-founder Eric Demuth had previously ruled out the London Stock Exchange because of concerns regarding market depth.

The former Bitpanda chief during an interview explained,

“Everyone is moving away from the LSE right now.”

He pointed to other fintech firms like Wise, which recently moved its primary listing from London to New York, as a sign of the current trend.

📉 Bitpanda has abandoned its IPO in London

The cryptocurrency exchange Bitpanda will not go public in London, citing the low liquidity of the London Stock Exchange.

According to the company's co-founder Eric Demuth, the listing may take place in Frankfurt or New York, but the… pic.twitter.com/KFtrkC1PEd

— Crypto Research Network (@CrResearchNet) August 26, 2025

While New York was also considered, Bitpanda eventually settled on Frankfurt to lean into its identity as a dominant European firm.

With a 60 percent market share in Austria, the company is positioning itself as a local champion that understands the regulatory landscape of the European Union better than its international rivals.

Will Recent Profitability Support A Five Billion Euro Valuation

Investors will be looking closely at Bitpanda’s books as it prepares for the 2026 debut.

The company reported a significant jump in revenue for 2024, reaching 393 million euros compared to 147.6 million euros the year before.

🚨 @Bitpanda IS GOING HUGE 🚨

✅Bitpanda hits €393 million in 2024 revenue.
✅Plans UK and European expansion.
✅Regulatory approvals strengthen regional presence.

Are you guys familiar with this exchange yet? 👀 pic.twitter.com/ekc1B9dIxG

— Crypto Virtuos (@CryptoVirtuos) August 5, 2025

This growth has been accompanied by a shift in how the company reports its earnings to better align with international standards.

In 2025, Bitpanda began focusing on adjusted EBITDA as its primary performance indicator, achieving a margin of over 30 percent.

This transparency is intended to show potential shareholders that the platform is not just growing in user numbers, which now exceed 7 million, but is also a sustainable and profitable business.

The company’s ability to secure a MiCA licence in Germany and approval from the UK Financial Conduct Authority has also added a layer of regulatory security that many of its competitors lack.

A New Leadership Team Takes The Reins For The IPO

Ahead of the planned listing, Bitpanda has restructured its top-level management to better handle the responsibilities of a public company.

In 2025, co-founder Eric Demuth stepped back from the day-to-day operations as co-CEO to become the Executive Chairman of the Board.

10 years of @Bitpanda_global with the best co-founders I could have hoped for.

I’ve never been a big believer in the concept of “luck.” For me, luck is just about optimizing your chances, maximizing the expected value of an outcome. But if I’m honest, meeting @christiant5r and… pic.twitter.com/zvDhF6bXmf

— Eric Demuth (@eric_demuth) January 3, 2025

This left Lukas Enzersdorfer-Konrad as the sole CEO to lead the company through its stock market debut.

The other original founders, Paul Klanschek and Christian Trummer, had already moved out of executive roles.

This shift provides the company with a more traditional corporate structure that investors often prefer during an IPO.

The new leadership is tasked with maintaining Bitpanda's momentum as it competes for dominance against global exchanges like Binance and Kraken.

How Will The 2026 Crypto IPO Wave Impact The Market

Bitpanda is not the only digital asset firm trying to catch the current wave of market optimism.

Several major players are lining up for their own debuts in 2026, encouraged by a friendlier regulatory environment in the United States and the clear MiCA guidelines in Europe.

🚨 Crypto is going mainstream faster than most expect.

2026 is shaping up to be a massive year for crypto IPOs 👀

1️⃣ Kraken
2️⃣ ConsenSys
3️⃣ BitGo
4️⃣ Animoca
5️⃣ Ledger
6️⃣ Bithumb

Above are lining up with $35B+ in combined valuations. pic.twitter.com/udVijDPj81

— Lucky (@LLuciano_BTC) December 29, 2025

Kraken has already filed a draft registration for an IPO that could value it at 20 billion dollars, while other firms like tZERO and Grayscale Investments are also making moves.

However, the performance of crypto companies that went public in 2025, such as Circle and Gemini, has been varied.

While some saw high initial trading prices, many later saw their shares retreat.

Bitpanda is hoping that its strong footing in the European market and its recent profitability will help it avoid the volatility that affected earlier crypto listings.

data.title: A Game Between Freedom and Clarity: A Fundamental Study of Poland's Crypto Tax System and Regulatory Framework
data.description:
1. Introduction

In late 2025, Poland was embroiled in a fierce debate surrounding a cryptocurrency regulatory bill. According to an official Polish government announcement, on December 9, 2025, the Council of Ministers approved a draft law on the cryptocurrency market submitted by the Minister of Finance and Economy. This bill, which had been rejected by the President on December 2, was resubmitted with the same wording. This stalemate made Poland one of the few EU countries that had not yet completed its domestic legislation supporting the Crypto-Asset Market Regulation Act (MiCA).
